beats.text: Before you fill a single empty cell, the first question is why that value went missing in the first place.
Rubin's framework sorts the reason into three cases by what the probability of being missing actually depends on.
MCAR means the holes are pure coincidence; missingness depends on nothing you observed or hid.
MAR means missingness depends only on data you did record, like older respondents skipping the income question.
MNAR means the value hides itself; high earners refuse to state income precisely because it is high.
You cannot test MAR against MNAR from data alone, so you assume MAR and probe sensitivity with what-if models.

visual.label: mechanisms, only one is testable from data

concepts.title: Simple imputation — and what it costs

beats.text: The reflex fix replaces every missing entry with one constant: the mean, the median, or the mode.

visual.sub: One constant per column, and its hidden cost

beats.text: Mean imputation fills every hole with the average of the observed entries, which is one line of code.

visual.note: every hole gets the same number

beats.text: It commits two quiet crimes: it shrinks the variance and it destroys the column's correlations.

visual.note: variance scales by the share of real data

beats.text: Mean imputing the column 2, 4, NA, 8 fills the hole with the average of the three observed values.
The collapse is linear in the missing fraction, so a column that is forty percent empty loses forty percent of its spread.
Median and mode share the flaw of a single constant, but resist outliers and apply to categories.

concepts.title: kNN imputation: borrow from your neighbours

beats.text: The first real upgrade stops filling with a global constant and starts filling with a local one.

visual.sub: Fill each hole from its nearest neighbours

beats.text: k-nearest-neighbour imputation finds the k most similar rows and fills the hole with their average.
Distance is measured only over columns both rows observe, so missingness never poisons the metric.
Because each fill is conditioned on a local neighbourhood, kNN preserves the correlation a flat mean erases.
Small k is flexible but noisy; large k smooths toward the global mean and reintroduces shrinkage.
You must scale features first, or the nearest neighbours become an artifact of whichever column has the largest units.

visual.label: O(n^2) scoring cost in the naive form

concepts.title: MICE: multiple imputation by chained equations

beats.text: Every method so far fills one best guess and proceeds as if it were ground truth, hiding all uncertainty.
Multiple imputation generates several complete datasets with different fills, analyses each, and pools the results.

visual.label: imputations typically beat one perfect fill

beats.text: Each sweep regresses every missing column on all the others and draws fresh imputations from that conditional model.

visual.tex: x^{(t+1)}_{\cdot c\,\in\,\text{mis}} \sim P\!\left(x_{\cdot c} \mid x_{\cdot 1}, \ldots, x_{\cdot p};\ \hat{\theta}_c\right)

beats.text: Drawing from the distribution rather than its mean is what injects the honest uncertainty MICE exists to preserve.
Rubin's rules pool the estimates by adding within-imputation and between-imputation variance into one total.

visual.note: between-imputation spread B is what single fills throw away

beats.text: Chained equations have no joint-distribution guarantee, yet they are the default in R's mice and scikit-learn.

concepts.title: Model-based & indicator strategies; choosing in practice

beats.text: Two more tools round out the kit: model-based imputation and the missing-indicator method.

visual.sub: Model-based fills, indicators, and the rules

beats.text: Model-based imputation fits one probabilistic model of the whole matrix and reads the missing values off it.
The missing-indicator method adds a binary was-this-missing column, which is often predictive under MNAR.
Always impute inside the cross-validation fold, never before, or test information leaks into training.
Match the method to the mechanism: MCAR forgives anything, MAR rewards conditioning, MNAR demands an explicit model.
Gradient-boosted trees handle NaN natively by learning a default split, often the strongest baseline of all.
